- pdf-viewer:open: Open a PDF in the interactive viewer
- pdf-viewer:sign: Place a signature or initials image on a PDF
- pdf-viewer:fill-form: Fill PDF form fields interactively with live visual feedback
- pdf-viewer:annotate: Collaboratively annotate a PDF — propose markup, review together, iterate
- anthropic-skills:consolidate-memory: Reflective pass over your memory files — merge duplicates, fix stale facts, prune the index.
- anthropic-skills:skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- anthropic-skills:pdf: Use this skill whenever the user wants to do anything with PDF files. This includes reading or extracting text/tables from PDFs, combining or merging multiple PDFs into one, splitting PDFs apart, rotating pages, adding watermarks, creating new PDFs, filling PDF forms, encrypting/decrypting PDFs, extracting images, and OCR on scanned PDFs to make them searchable. If the user mentions a .pdf file or asks to produce one, use this skill.

Triggers include: any mention of 'Word doc', 'word document', '.docx', or requests to produce professional documents with formatting like tables of contents, headings, page numbers, or letterheads. Also use when extracting or reorganizing content from .docx files, inserting or replacing images in documents, performing find-and-replace in Word files, working with tracked changes or comments, or converting content into a polished Word document. If the user asks for a 'report', 'memo', 'letter', 'template', or similar deliverable as a Word or .docx file, use this skill. Do NOT use for PDFs, spreadsheets, Google Docs, or general coding tasks unrelated to document generation.
- anthropic-skills:pptx: Use this skill any time a .pptx file is involved in any way — as input, output, or both. This includes: creating slide decks, pitch decks, or presentations; reading, parsing, or extracting text from any .pptx file (even if the extracted content will be used elsewhere, like in an email or summary); editing, modifying, or updating existing presentations; combining or splitting slide files; working with templates, layouts, speaker notes, or comments. Trigger whenever the user mentions "deck," "slides," "presentation," or references a .pptx filename, regardless of what they plan to do with the content afterward. If a .pptx file needs to be opened, created, or touched, use this skill.

Also runs WCAG accessibility scoring. Use when user says 'extract design', 'get design system', 'design language', 'design tokens', 'what colors/fonts does this site use', or '/extract-design'.

- pdf-viewer:view-pdf: Interactive PDF viewer. Use when the user wants to open, show, or view a PDF and collaborate on it visually — annotate, highlight, stamp, fill form fields, place signature/initials, or review markup together. Not for summarization or text extraction (use native Read instead).
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".

- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.

+//
+//  RadiationLevelChartView.swift
+//  Radiacode
+//
+//  Created by Matvey Kozin on 06.02.2024.
+//
+
+import SwiftUI
+import DGCharts
+import Combine
+
+struct RadiationLevelChartView: UIViewRepresentable {
+    
+    let rawData: [RadiationLevelChartDataModel]
+    let purifiedData: [RadiationLevelChartDataModel]
+    let timeRange: RadiationLevelOptions.TimeRange
+    @Binding var scale: RadiationLevelOptions.Scale
+    
+    let radiationType: RadiationType.Level
+    let alarms: Alarm.Level?
+    let mainSourceForCalculating: Bool
+    @Binding var indexes: [Int]
+
+    func makeUIView(context: Context) -> LineChartView {
+        let chartView = LineChartView.makeRadiationLevelChart(
+            rawData: rawData,
+            purifiedData: purifiedData,
+            timeRange: timeRange,
+            scale: scale,
+            color: NSUIColor(radiationType.accentColor),
+            coordinator: context.coordinator,
+            indexes: $indexes,
+            mainSourceForCalculating: mainSourceForCalculating
+        )
+        
+        drawLines(chart: chartView)
+        context.coordinator.cachedAlarms = alarms
+        
+        return chartView
+    }
+    
+    func updateUIView(_ uiView: LineChartView, context: Context) {
+        let coordinator = context.coordinator
+        uiView.updateData(rawData: rawData, purifiedData: purifiedData, coordinator: coordinator)
+        let updateMoving = uiView.updateMoving(scale: scale, timeRange: timeRange, coordinator: coordinator)
+        
+        var needUpdate = false
+        if coordinator.cachedAlarms != alarms {
+            coordinator.cachedAlarms = alarms
+            uiView.rightAxis.removeAllLimitLines()
+            drawLines(chart: uiView)
+            needUpdate = true
+        }
+        
+        if updateMoving || needUpdate {
+            uiView.notifyDataSetChanged()
+        }
+    }
+    
+    func makeCoordinator() -> RadiationLevelChartCoordinator {
+        RadiationLevelChartCoordinator(scale: $scale)
+    }
+
+    private func drawLines(chart: LineChartView) {
+        if let lvl1 = alarms?.level1 {
+            chart.rightAxis.addLimitLine(limitLine(color: .accentYellow, value: lvl1))
+        }
+        
+        if let lvl2 = alarms?.level2 {
+            chart.rightAxis.addLimitLine(limitLine(color: .accentRed, value: lvl2))
+        }
+    }
+    
+    private func limitLine(color: Color, value: Decimal) -> ChartLimitLine {
+        let limitLine = ChartLimitLine(limit: value.doubleValue)
+        limitLine.lineColor = NSUIColor(color)
+        limitLine.lineWidth = 0.5
+        return limitLine
+    }
+}

There's the critical bug! 

`RadiationLevelChartDataModel` implements `Hashable` with synthesized hash (which uses ALL fields including `count` and `time`), but `==` only checks `time`. This violates the contract: if `a == b`, then `a.hashValue == b.hashValue`. But since `==` only checks `time`, two items with the same `time` but different `count` are equal, but their hash values would be different (since hash includes `count`).

This breaks Set-based lookup! When you do:
```swift
let excludeArray = Set(coordinator.cachedRawData)


let addedObjects = rawData.filter { !excludeArray.contains($0) }
```

The `contains` check uses the hash to find the bucket, then verifies equality. But if the `count` value has changed for the same timestamp, the hash will differ, so the Set won't find the match even though the two objects are considered equal. For instance, if the cached data has a radiation level of 2.35 at timestamp 12345 and the new data has 2.40 at the same timestamp, they're equal by the `==` operator but have different hashes, causing the filter to incorrectly treat it as a new object.

The old array-based approach used direct equality checking, which only compared timestamps, so it correctly identified duplicates. The Set optimization broke this because the synthesized hash includes both count and time, while equality only checks time—when the count updates but the timestamp stays the same, the hash changes and Set.contains fails to recognize it as a duplicate.

The fix is straightforward: revert to using the array's `.contains` method instead of converting to a Set, which restores the time-only comparison logic. This raises a question though—if rawData needed this fix, why wasn't the same issue affecting purifiedData in the original code?

- The averaging output is `count: avg_of_window, time: rawData[startIndex].time`

So: out-of-order insertion causes old averaged points to disappear and new ones to appear. 

When a new raw point gets inserted at position K, the averaged points before K/2 stay unchanged since their windows don't include it, but everything from K/2 onward shifts—their midpoint indices increment by 1, pointing to different raw data points and thus different timestamps.

Since the old cached averaged points have different times than the newly computed ones, they're treated as distinct objects. The old ones disappear from the recalculated array but never get removed from the chart dataset, while the new ones get added, leaving ghost points behind.

Out-of-order insertion seems unlikely with a radiation sensor delivering data sequentially, but the real issue is probably that `averagingRawData` gets completely recomputed from scratch each time new data arrives, creating an entirely new array of objects.

Using a Set-based approach to track which objects are already in the dataset works well—when a new point arrives, only the genuinely new averaged point gets added to the chart. But when the code tries to manage the size limit by removing the oldest entry...

Using a Set to filter added objects doesn't solve this because it only prevents duplicates—it doesn't remove stale entries that were already drawn. Let me trace through a concrete example: if cachedRawData has points A, B, and C, and rawData shifts to B, C, and D, then filtering for what's not in the cache would correctly identify only D as new. But the old point A never gets removed from the set, so it lingers even though it's no longer part of the active window.

With the Set approach using a synthesized hash based on count and timestamp, the behavior should be identical if the counts remain stable—both methods would add D and keep A around. But if the hash doesn't properly account for all the distinguishing factors, the Set might fail to recognize duplicates or stale entries that the array method would catch.

When `removeFirst` shifts the window, the averaging calculation for each point changes because the sum and divisor shift, which could alter the count values and break the hash-based deduplication in the Set approach. When the oldest raw data point gets removed, every averaged point shifts by one sample in the window, which means both their count values and timestamps change. Since raw samples arrive at 2 per second, this shifts each averaged point's time by 0.5 seconds later than before.

The problem with using a Set-based lookup is that the hash depends on both the count and time—so when a point's count changes due to the window shift, its hash no longer matches what's in the exclude set. With the original array-based approach using equality checks, it only compares the time value, so shifted points with different counts are still correctly identified as already existing and skipped.
